coredata.dc:title: Electrolytic exfoliation synthesis of boron doped graphene quantum dots: a new luminescent material for electrochemiluminescence detection of oncogene microRNA-20a
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tingting
Zhao, Huimin
Fan, Gaofeng
Li, Yaxuan
Li, Li
Quan, Xie
coredata.dc:description: In this work, boron doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) with the atomic percentage of boron (B) of 0.67-2.26% were synthesized by electrolytic exfoliation of B doped graphene rods. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of BGQDs confirmed that the B atoms have been successfully incorporated into the GQDs. Characterizations by transmission electron microscopy, photoluminescence (PL) spectra and UV-visible spectra demonstrated that BGQDs had a consistent lattice spacing with the plane of graphene and possessed good luminous performance. The PL stability, the electrochemical impedance and Zeta potential test indicated that the 60 days-PL stability of BGQDs was improved by 51% and the resistance of BGQDs was decreased by 11% compared with that of the pure GQDs when 1.29% (atomic percentage) of B was doped. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing results showed that the ECL sensor with BGQDs as luminescent material exhibited an excellent analytical property for the detection of miRNA-20a in a linear range of 0.1-1×104 pM with a quantification limit of 0.1 pM. Specificity tests showed that this ECL sensor could distinguish the target miRNAs-20a with several similar bases strand. The obtained results in this study prove that the proposed ECL sensor provides a high sensitively, specifically and simply platform for detection of miRNAs-20a.
coredata.dcterms:subject: boron doping
graphene quantum dots
microRNAs
electrochemiluminescence sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ECL probe: BGQDs tagged DNA
ECL behaviors and EIS characterization of the ECL electrode

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Changjiang Scholar Program of Chinese Ministry of Education
Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Research Project of Chinese Ministry of Education (No113017A). Thanks Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (IRT_13R05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation processes of ECL electrode and the detection procedures of the ECL sensor.

(A) TEM images of BGQDs. XPS of full range (B), (inset: local amplification from 200 to 180eV), B1s (C) and C1s (D) spectra of BGQDs. (E) Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra (the left coordinates) and UV/Vis absorption spectra (Abs) (the right coordinates) of the BGQDs. (F) PL decay rate of BGQDs with the different B atomic percentage. The PL decay rate (%)=[(PL0-PL30/60)/PL0]×100%. (G) EIS of BCQDs with different B atomic percentage in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.005M [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4.

(A) The ECL signals of the different modified electrodes in 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M KCl and 0.05M K2S2O8, and the ECL electrode was scanned from 0.7V to 1.15V. Scan rate, 0.1Vs−1. The voltage of PMT was 600V. (B) EIS of the different modified electrodes in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.005M [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4. The frequency range is between 0.1 and 1,000,000Hz with signal amplitude of 0V.

(A) Effects of pH value of electrolyte solution and (B) hybridization time of miRNA-20a on the response of ECL sensor for the detection of 5nM miRNA-20a (n=3) in 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M KCl and 0.05M K2S2O8 and the ECL electrode was scanned from 0.7V to 1.15V. Scan rate, 0.1Vs−1. The voltage of PMT was 600V.

(A) ECL responses of the proposed ECL sensor to different concentrations of miRNA-20a (0, 0.1 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 250, 500, 1×103, 5×103, 1×104 and 5×104 pM): in 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M KCl and 0.05M K2S2O8 and the ECL electrode was scanned from 0.7V to 1.15V. Scan rate, 0.1Vs−1. The voltage of PMT was 600V. (B) The calibration curve for miRNA-20a detection (n=3).

The selectivity of the proposed ECL sensor of miRNA-20a, single-base mismatch miRNA (a), three-base mismatch miRNA (b) and non-complementary miRNA (c), respectively (n=3). All measurements were performed in 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M KCl and 0.05M K2S2O8 and the ECL electrode was scanned from 0.7V to 1.15V. Scan rate, 0.1Vs−1. The voltage of PMT was 600V.

The ECL intensities of sixteen consecutive cycles of the proposed ECL sensor incubated with 100 pM miRNA-20a in 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M KCl and 0.05M K2S2O8 and the ECL electrode was scanned from 0.7V to 1.15V. Scan rate, 0.1Vs−1. The voltage of PMT was 600V.
Zeta potential of BGQDs with different amount of boron.
Comparison between the described ECL sensor and referenced methods for miRNAs detection.
The relative standard deviation value from the intra- and inter-assay of the proposed ECL sensor.
Determination of miRNA-20a in extracellular secretion and recoveries of the samples with different concentrations of miRNA-20a.

head.ce:title: Electrolytic exfoliation synthesis of boron doped graphene quantum dots: a new luminescent material for electrochemiluminescence detection of oncogene microRNA-20a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering, Ministry of Education, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, boron doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) with the atomic percentage of boron (B) of 0.67-2.26% were synthesized by electrolytic exfoliation of B doped graphene rods. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of BGQDs confirmed that the B atoms have been successfully incorporated into the GQDs. Characterizations by transmission electron microscopy, photoluminescence (PL) spectra and UV-visible spectra demonstrated that BGQDs had a consistent lattice spacing with the plane of graphene and possessed good luminous performance. The PL stability, the electrochemical impedance and Zeta potential test indicated that the 60 days-PL stability of BGQDs was improved by 51% and the resistance of BGQDs was decreased by 11% compared with that of the pure GQDs when 1.29% (atomic percentage) of B was doped. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing results showed that the ECL sensor with BGQDs as luminescent material exhibited an excellent analytical property for the detection of miRNA-20a in a linear range of 0.1-1×104 pM with a quantification limit of 0.1 pM. Specificity tests showed that this ECL sensor could distinguish the target miRNAs-20a with several similar bases strand. The obtained results in this study prove that the proposed ECL sensor provides a high sensitively, specifically and simply platform for detection of miRNAs-20a.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are a class of endogenously expressed, single-stranded and non-protein-coding RNAs [1]. They often contain approximately 19-23 nucleotides which function in post-transcriptional regulation of gene expression [2]. Research shows that more than 30% of the protein-coding genes may become the target genes of miRNAs and 50% of miRNAs are highly expressed in cancer related area or fragile sites in the genome expression [3,4]. Many researchers consider that miRNAs can be used as a tumor biomarker [5,6]. Therefore, miRNAs profiling methods have show great potential in basic and clinical research on tumor as well as for the evaluation of drug efficiency [7]. Develop a rapid and sensitive method for identification and quantification of miRNAs largely remains a technical challenge due to the emerging diagnostic value and low abundance of miRNAs [8]. The conventional miRNAs detection techniques mainly includes polymerase-chain reaction (PCR) [9], northern blotting [10] and microarray [11]. Though these methods are reliable, their applications are limited due to their disadvantages, for example, they are complicated, expensive, time consuming and less sensitivity. Recently, several detection systems have been developed for the determination of miRNAs, such as isothermal exponential amplification reaction (EXPAR) [12], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [13] and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [14]. In addition to these methods, a series of electrochemical sensors have been developed for detection of miRNAs [15,16]. For example, a electrochemical sensor based on enzymatic gap hybridization assay to detect miRNA-10a, miRNA-16, and miRNA-21 was fabricated by Pöhlmann and Sprinzl [17]. Bettazzi et al. [18] reported an electrochemical sensor for miRNA-222 detection by using a magnetic bead-based bioassay. These methods are sensitive but their applications are still limited due to the complex operation process of transcribed miRNAs probe and costly preparation of labeled nanomaterials [19]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple and efficient method for miRNAs detection.

The electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor has attracted growing attention due to its intrinsic advantages, such as high sensitivity, good controllability and fast response [20,21]. The ECL material is one of the important parts of a ECL sensor which influences the performance of the sensor. The traditional ECL materials, luminal and ruthenium compounds, are highly toxic to organism, which limit their application. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a kind of zero-dimensional nanomaterial obtained from graphene which have some excellent characteristics of large surface area, good surface grafting by π-π conjugated network [22,23]. Moreover, due to their exceptional advantages of resistance to photobleaching, excellent solubility and chemical inertness, they are considered as highly promising bionanomaterials [24,25]. And they are widely used in fluorescent sensors [26], bioimaging [27], fuel cells [28] and photovoltaic devices [29]. In particular, the fascinating merits of GQDs, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, in comparison to traditional ECL materials (luminal and ruthenium compounds), make them attract great attention as a luminescent material in ECL sensor [30,31]. Recently, some researchers have found that the intrinsic properties of carbon materials can be effectively tuned by doping with heteroatom, consequently producing new phenomena and unexpected characteristics [32]. Boron (B) is the neighbor chemical element of carbon in the periodic table and has similar valence orbital with that of carbon. Piazza et al. [33] demonstrated that extended single-atom layer boron sheets can form highly stable quasiplanar boron cluster with a central hexagonal hole like graphene structures. Additionally, researches on boron-doped diamond electrode (BDD) have showed that boron doping is conduced to improve the stability of diamond electrode and reduce the electrical resistance of diamond electrode in a great extent [34–36]. Therefore, it is predictable that doping with B may take an effect on the chemical and electronic characteristics of the GQDs. Recently, Fan and his co-workers reported an approach of electrochemical reaction for synthesizing B doped GQDs (BGQDs) which showed to be highly fluorescent in borax (Na2B4O7·10H2O) solution and could formed fluorescent crystals after evaporation [37]. In addition, Zhang et al. [38] reported an hydrothermal approach for synthesizing BGQDs and used BGQDs as a new photoluminescence (PL) probe for glucose sensing. The study result shows that the doping of boron can introduce the GQDs to a new kind of surface state and offer great scientific insights to the PL enhancement mechanism with treatment of glucose.

The common electrochemical process for preparation of heteroatom doped GQDs was achieved in a mixture of heteroatom salt with electrolyte using graphite rods as both anode and cathode. Herein, the uniformity of obtained heteroatom doped GQDs was influenced by the change of electrolyte concentration during this process. While, BGQDs used in the following experiment was synthesized by electrolytic exfoliation of B doped graphene rods, which avoided the problem of electrolyte concentration change in common electrochemical process, leading to the increase of the uniformity of BGQDs. The PL stability and the electrical activity of BGQDs with the different doping amounts of B were evaluated. Based on the excellent luminescent property of BGQDs, we designed an ECL sensor for quantitative detecting of miRNA-20a which involved in at least three types of solid cancers [39]. The ECL probe, BGQDs tagged hairpin DNA, could self-assemble on the surface of the Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) modified platinum (Pt) electrode. The spatial structure of hairpin DNA was transformed after hybridization with a target miRNA-20a, resulting in the changed ECL signal. The ECL signal dynamically depended on the amount of target miRNA-20a, which allowed the quantitative assay of the miRNA-20a. The preparation processes of ECL electrode and the detection procedures of the ECL sensor were illustrated in Scheme 1 .

Graphite powder (99%) was obtained from Shenzhen Nanotechnology Port. Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). Boric acid (H3BO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), H2O2, and NaOH were purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Ethyl-3-(dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma (America). Potassiumpersulfate (K2S2O8) and KCl were bought from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin,China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1M, pH=7.4) was prepared by mixing the stock solutions of Na2HPO4·12H2O (0.1M) and NaH2PO4·2H2O (0.1M). Ultrapure water, which was purified using a Millipore water system (resistivity > 18.0 MΩ cm−1, Laikie Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), was used throughout the experiments. The thiolated hairpin DNA probe, miRNA-20a, single-base mismatch miRNA, three-base mismatch miRNA and non-complementary miRNA were obtained from Takara Biotechnology (Dalian) Co., Ltd. (Dalian, China) with HPLC purification.
Thiolated hairpin DNA:
5′-SH-(CH)6-CCACGCTACCTGCACTATAAGCACTTTACGTGG-(CH2)6-NH2-3′
miRNA-20a [40]:
5′-UAAAGUGCUUAUAGUGCAGGUAG-3′
single-base mismatch miRNA:
5′-UAAAGUGCUUAUGGUGCAGGUAG-3′
three-base mismatch miRNA:
5′-UAAAGAGCUUAUGGUGCAGUUAG-3′
non-complementary miRNA:
5′-GUCCAUAAAGGCGAAUAAUGCCU-3′

The ECL emission was detected using a MPI-B multi-parameter chemiluminescence analysis test system (Xi’an Remax Analysis Instrument Co., Ltd., Xi’an, China) with a three-electrode system, which consisted of a bare or modified Pt electrode (1mm-diameter) as the working electrode, silver-silver chloride electrode (Ag/AgCl, saturated KCl) as the reference electrode and a Pt wire as the auxiliary electrode. Unless noted, the voltage of photomultiplier tube (PMT) was 600V. The morphology of sample was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 20, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a VG ESCALAB 250 spectrometer using a nonmonochromatized Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6eV). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer (Japan). UV-visible spectra were examined by a Jasco V-550 spectrometer (Japan). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured by a CHI 660 electrochemical analyzer (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, Shanghai, China) in a 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.005M [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4.

Preparation of boron doped graphene rods: First, graphite oxide was synthesized using the modified Hummers method [41]: Briefly, 1g graphite powder was added into 23mL concentrated H2SO4 under vigorous stirring in an ice bath. Subsequently, 3g KMnO4 was slowly added under the same condition and continuely sonicated for 24h. After that, added 46mL H2O into the above mixture and rested for 15minutes. Following this step, 140mL ultrapure water and 10mL H2O2 (30%) were added to terminate the reaction. Then, the mixture was centrifugated and washed with diluted HCl (5%) and ultrapure water, respectively, to obtain graphite oxide. Second, boron doped graphene rods were synthesized through thermal annealing graphite oxide in the presence of H3BO3. The specific process was as follows: H3BO3 was dissolved in 25ml as-prepared graphite oxide. The resulted mixture was then transferred into a glass tube and treated at 80°C for 12h to obtain a rod of boron doped graphene. Finally, the boron doped graphene rods were washed by ultrapure water followed by vacuum freeze-dry. Pure graphene rods were also synthesized as mentioned above, only without adding H3BO3.

Preparation of BGQDs: Synthesis of BGQDs was performed according to the reported procedure of electrochemical synthesis of GQDs [42]. Briefly, The as-prepared boron doped graphene rods and graphite rods (diameter about 0.5cm) were used as anode and cathode, respectively. The electrolyte was a 10mL mixture of ethanol and H2O (volume ratio=99.5: 0.5) with 0.01g NaOH. BGQDs were prepared with a current intensity in the range of 200-250mAcm−2. After 2h electrochemical reaction, the BGQDs were collected by centrifuging the resulting solution at 9×103 rpm to remove graphene particles. Then, the obtained solution was dialyzed over ultrapure water in a dialysis bag (1×103 Da) for 24h to remove the electrolyte of salts. Afterwards, the purified BGQDs solution was stored at 4°C. For comparison, GQDs was also prepared using a similar procedure with pure graphene rods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ECL probe: BGQDs tagged DNA
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare ECL probe BGQDs tagged DNA, as-prepared BGQDs were of carboxylation by adding 0.015g of NaOH and 0.025g of ClCH2COOH into 0.5mL of BGQDs solution and then sonicating for 3h (frequency: 40 KHz, power: 200W) [43]. Then, 400μL NHS (150mg/ml) and 250μL EDC (10mg/ml) were added as coupling reagents and reacted for 30min. After that, 50μL DNA (100μM) was added into the above mixture and gently stirred overnight. The mixture were centrifuged and the final precipitate were BGQDs-DNA. Finally, the prepared BGQDs-DNA were diluted with 0.1M Tris-EDTA (TE) buffer (pH 8.0) and stored at 4°C for use.

A Pt electrode (1 mm-diameter) was polished with 50nm and 0.5μm alumina slurry on a polishing cloth and ultrasonically washed in ethanol and ultrapure water for a few minutes, respectively. 2μL of AuNPs suspension was dropped on the pretreated Pt electrode (noted as Pt/Au) and naturally dried at room temperature. Subsequently, 2μL of the BGQDs-DNA solution was dropped on the surface of Pt/Au electrode and allowed to stand for 2h to connect BGQDs-DNA onto the Pt/Au electrode [44]. The electrode was rinsed with 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4) to remove excess BGQDs-DNA, and the ECL electrode was obtained and noted as Pt/Au/BGQDs-DNA.

The Pt/Au/BGQDs-DNA electrode was immersed in a 50μL of 0.1M Tris-HCl solutions, which contained various concentrations of miRNA-20a. The reaction was performed at 37°C for 60min in a water bath. Then, the electrode was rinsed with 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4) (noted as Pt/Au/BGQDs-DNA/miRNA-20a). The modified electrode was scanned in 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.4) which contained 0.1M KCl and 0.05M K2S2O8. The PMT voltage was maintained at 600V, and the potential range was 0.7-1.15V with a scan rate of 0.1Vs−1.
The performance of the ECL sensor for the detection of miRNA-20a was analyzed in terms of sensor linear range, detection limit, specificity, stability and reproducibility.

The TEM image shows that the size of BGQDs was 5-10nm in diameter, and the lattice spacing was 0.25nm corresponding to the lattice constant in the plane of graphene (Fig. 1 A). It reveals that highly crystalline BGQDs was synthesized by electrolytic exfoliation of B doped graphene rods. To explore the content and configuration of BGQDs, XPS characterization of BGQDs was analyzed. The full range XPS (Fig. 1B) analysis of BGQDs sample clearly shows the corresponding B1s, C1s and O1s peaks were located at 191.8eV, 284eV and 532eV, and the presence of B, C and O with atomic percentages of 1.29%, 80.44% and 18.26%, respectively. In the high-resolution B1s spectrum (Fig. 1C), the peak centered at 190.7eV was assigned to the structure of B atoms bonding to C and O atoms (BC2O). The peaks at 191.6eV and 192.7eV correspond to the structure of B atoms surrounded by C and O atoms (BCO2) and boron oxide (B2O3) [45,46], respectively. In the C1s photoemission data (Fig. 1D), the main peak at 284.8eV was related to C sp2 [47]. In addition, two high resolution C1s spectrums were confirmed: the peaks at 285.1eV and 286.3 were related to C sp3 and C-O, respectively, one lower intensity components at 288.6eV was associated to C=O [48,49]. This confirmed that the B atoms coming from H3BO3 have been successfully incorporated into the GQDs. The PL spectra and UV/vis absorption were used to evaluate the photoluminescence properties of BGQDs (Fig. 1E). In the UV/vis absorption spectrum of BGQDs aqueous solution, there was a typical absorption peak at 230nm which could be assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [50]. Moreover, the two absorption peak at 280 and 350nm were conformed to be n-π* absorption bands [51]. In the PL spectra of BGQDs aqueous solution, two wavelength regions peaking were detected at 460 and 530nm. The peak at 460nm was dominated when excited at wavelengths from 300 to 360nm. When changed excitation wavelengths from 380 to 420nm the emission wavelengths shifted to 530nm. The location of PL emission peak of BGQDs was showed to be consistent with reported GQDs [52]. It indicates that the location of PL emission peak of BGQDs has not been changed by B doped.

The PL stability and the electrical activity are two important indicators of QDs properties. Fig. 1F and Fig. 1G show the PL decay rates and the EIS of BGQDs with different doping amounts of B (in atomic percentage), respectively. From Fig. 1G we can clearly see that after stored at 4 °C over 30 and 60 days, the PL decay rates of BGQDs with the different doping amount of B were different. After 30 and 60 days, the lowest PL decay rate were 0.96% and 1.46%, respectively, which appeared by doping B with atomic percentage of 1.29%. It means that the PL stability of BGQDs with the B atomic percentage of 1.29% was the highest, and the 60 days-PL stability of BGQDs was improved by 51% compared with that of the pure GQDs. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 1H, with the different atomic percentages of B, the EIS of BGQDs were different. When the atomic percentage of B increased from 0 to 1.29%, the EIS of BGQDs decreased with the increase of the atomic percentage of B. And when doping B with atomic percentage of 1.29%, the EIS of BGQDs was decreased by 11% compared with that of the pure GQDs. In consideration that EIS difference could be cause by the change of surface charges, the Zeta potential of BGQDs with different amount of boron were detected and the date were given in Table 1 . The max surface charges of -40.3 was appeared by doping B with atomic percentage of 1.29%. The trend of Zeta potential data was in accord with that of the EIS results, which explained the reason of EIS change. The above experiments clearly shows that the boron doping transformed the PL stability and the EIS of the BGQDs, and the BGQDs was of its best behavior at a doping B with atomic percentage of 1.29%, with the 60 days-PL stability and EIS of the BGQDs reaching the best.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL behaviors and EIS characterization of the ECL electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
ECL behaviors of the ECL electrode were recorded in 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M KCl and 0.05M K2S2O8, scanned from 0.7V to 1.15V (Fig. 2 A). For Pt electrode and Pt/Au electrode, there was no ECL emission. After the modification with BGQDs-DNA, the ECL intensity obviously increased. It was due to the ECL reaction between BGQDs and K2S2O8. The ECL behaviors of BGQDs was similar to that of GQDs. Consequently, the possible ECL mechanism of BGQDs was as follows: firstly, the electrochemical reduction of S2O8 2− produced strongly oxidation state SO4 •−, simultaneously, BGQDs•− radicals was produced by the electrochemical reduction of BGQDs. Then, an excited state (noted as BGQDs∗) was produced by the electron-transfer annihilation between SO4 •− and BGQDs•− radicals, finally emitted light. According to the research on GQDs of Li [54], the corresponding equations were proposed as follows:
B G Q D s +
e −
→ B G Q D
s
• −
S 2
O 8
2 −
+
e −
→
S 2
O 8
• 2 −
S 2
O 8
• 2 −
→ S
O 4
2 −
+ S
O 4
• −
B G Q D
s
• −
+ S
O 4
• −
→ B G Q D
s ∗
+ S
O 4
2 −
B G Q D
s ∗
→ B G Q D s + h ν

After hybridization with the target miRNA-20a the ECL intensity of Pt/Au/BGQDs-DNA/miRNA-20a was decreased. That because the folded structure of the hairpin DNA was transformed into a linear double helix after hybridization with the target miRNA-20a. And led the BGQDs tag to remove away from the electrode surface, causing the decrease of the ECL intensity. In addition, the increase of molecular weight or base pairs of ECL probe might cause its diffusion coefficient decreased, which could lead to the ECL efficiency of ECL probe decreased [55]. It confirms that the design was feasible for the detection of miRNA-20a.

EIS is an effective tool to investigate the assembly effect of the electrodes. Fig. 2B shows the EIS figures of different electrodes. Obvious differences in resistance were acquired for the electrodes at different stages of modification. The resistance of bare Pt electrode was 618.8Ω. After modified with AuNPs, the resistance of Pt/Au decreased to 407.5Ω. Modifying with BGQDs-DNA leaded to an increase of resistance to 544.2Ω. Finally, hybridization miRNA-20a with Pt/Au/BGQDs-DNA caused a considerable increase in resistance to 561.5Ω. This is because the protein layer as a inert electron and mass-transfer blocking layer hindered the diffusion of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− toward the electrode surface.

Considering the pH value may affect the activity of the biological molecules involved in the experiment, it is necessary to study the influence of pH value. To achieve an optimal ECL signal, the pH value of the electrolyte solution was investigated, shown in Fig. 3 A. A maximum value of the ECL intensity was achieved when the pH value of electrolyte solution was adjusted to 7.4. Furthermore, the pH 7.4 was also consistent with biological environment of human body, therefore, the pH 7.4 was selected as the optimal condition throughout this study. The hybridization time is also an important factor in the detecting process. The effect of the hybridization time of miRNA-20a on the ECL intensity was shown in Fig. 3B. The ECL intensity decreased with the increase of hybridization time from 30min to 60min. However, continue to prolong the reaction time, the ECL intensity did not changed obviously, which illustrates that the reaction approached equilibrium with 60min hybridization. Hence, the hybridization time of 60min was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

Under the optimal detection conditions, the sensitivity of the developed ECL sensor for the detection of miRNA-20a was investigated by varying the miRNA-20a concentrations range from 0.1pM to 5×104 pM. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the ECL intensity decreased with the increase of miRNA-20a concentration, and the dependence of ECL intensity on miRNA-20a concentration was presented in Fig. 4B. As a result, the ECL intensity decreased linearly with the increased miRNA-20a concentration ranging from 0.1 pM to 1×104 pM with a limit of quantity (LOQ) of 0.1 pM. The regression equation was
Δ
ECL = 88 .86 + 28 .11log x
with a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.95. And the detection limit and the linear range of the proposed ECL sensor comparing with previous methods were shown in Table 2 . Compared with these methods, the proposed ECL sensor can detect miRNAs sensitively with a simple process.

To investigated the repeatability of the proposed ECL sensor, the relative standard deviation value from the intra- and inter-assay were calculated in Table 3 . The relative standard deviation value of the intra-assay was 4.11%-6.25% and that of the inter-assay was 3.43%, which indicated that the proposed ECL sensor could meet the requirements of repeatability in practical application.

A great challenge of the miRNAs assay is to distinguish the target miRNAs with high similar bases strand. To investigate the selectivity of the proposed ECL sensor, three types of miRNA with similar bases strand were tested under the same experimental conditions, including single-base mismatched strand, three-base mismatched strand and non-complementary (Fig. 5 ). The ΔECL intensity of the similar bases strands were 6.5%-11.7% of that of miRNA-20a with the same concentration. And the ΔECL intensity of the similar bases strands were only 9.2%-14.6% of that of miRNA-20a when the concentration of similar bases strands were 100 times higher than that of target miRNA-20a. The results indicated that the proposed ECL sensor provides adequate specificity to assay miRNA-20a.

Fig. 6 displays the ECL emission of the proposed ECL sensor under 16 cycles of continuous potential scans for the detection of 100 pM of miRNA-20a. A stable ECL signal was observed with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.1%. The result indicates that the potential cycling stability of the sensor was excellent and suitable for ECL detection. The long-term storage stability of the ECL sensor was also investigated in a 60-days period. When the working electrode was stored at 4°C over 30 days, the response current decreased 3.9% of its initial response; in the following 30 days, it was decreased by 7.8%. It maintained more than 92.2% of the initial response after storing for 60 days, which demonstrates that the ECL sensor has a good stability with a life time of up to 60 days.

To investigate the practicability of the proposed ECL sensor in real samples, the sensor was further applied to analyze the concentrations of miRNA-20a in extracellular secretion. Recovery experiments were carried out by standard addition method. The results show that the recovery ranges were from 103.1% to 110.0% (Table 4 ). Therefore, the ECL sensor could be satisfactorily applied for the detection of miRNA-20a in real samples.

In conclusion, BGQDs were synthesized by electrolytic exfoliation of B doped graphene rods in this work. Characterizations by TEM, XPS, EIS and PL stability revealed that the electron transfer efficiency and PL stability were correlated with the doping amount of B. And compared with the pure GQDs, the resistance and 60 days-PL stability of BGQDs was decreased by 11% and improved by 51%, respectively, at presence of B with atomic percentages of 1.29%. The highly sensitive ECL sensor for miRNA-20a using the BGQDs as the luminescent substance was constructed. With the optimized detection conditions, the linear range of the ECL sensor was 0.1-1×104 pM with the determination coefficient of 0.95 and the detection limit of 0.1 pM. Meanwhile the ECL sensor showed excellent specificity and stability. Moreover, the experiment illustrates that BGQDs can serve as a new luminescent material in ECL detecting platform. It is believed that the B doping quantum dots have broad application prospects in sensing technology, and this work provides a platform with the excellent comprehensive performance for detecting of miRNAs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Research Project of Chinese Ministry of Education (No113017A). Thanks Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (IRT_13R05).
